title: btc-graph content source audit
objective: Audit Bitcoin educational sources and expand btc-graph with atomic concepts and strict prerequisite edges.
notes: Canonical source of truth for the content discovery loop. Update this JSON first, then regenerate the workbook.

sources.name: Bitcoin Optech Topics
sources.scope: Topic index covering protocol, wallet, Lightning, fee, relay, and operational concepts.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the index views plus individual topic pages for canonical naming, aliases, see-also links, and primary docs.
sources.notes: 157 topics plus 117 aliases make this a naming registry and gap detector more than a bulk node source. Implemented from this source in the current branch: protocol.x-only-public-keys, protocol.version-3-transaction-relay, protocol.tapscript, dev.taproot-psbt-fields, protocol.accidental-confiscation, dev.taproot-descriptors, dev.descriptor-wallet-policies, and dev.descriptors-in-psbt.

sources.name: BTC Transcripts Topics
sources.scope: Topic index into transcript-backed technical discussions and workshops.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track Topics, Categories, Types, and Sources as separate provenance surfaces before drilling into individual transcript families.
sources.notes: Use mainly for provenance and terminology normalization. Topic labels should not be imported 1:1.

sources.name: Bitcoin Dev Philosophy
sources.scope: Conceptual and systems-thinking source likely to surface bridge concepts and framing gaps.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track 9 chapter-level rows first, with later splits for dense chapters 5, 7, 8, and 9.
sources.notes: Heavy overlap with existing philosophy nodes, but strong for bridge concepts like validation sovereignty and consensus vs policy. Implemented from this source in the current branch: fundamentals.validation-sovereignty, protocol.consensus-vs-policy, and protocol.chain-split-risk.

sources.name: Mastering Bitcoin Book Map
sources.scope: Structured chapter map for foundational and implementation-oriented concept coverage.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track 14 chapter rows first, grouping chapters 4 to 12 as the primary extraction surface.
sources.notes: Already heavily reused in current nodes, so chapter titles are containers. Best current value is prerequisite ladders and bridge concepts. Implemented from this source in the current branch: protocol.light-client-trust-model, ops.full-node-vs-lightweight-wallet, custody.coin-selection, protocol.change-outputs, protocol.bech32m-addresses, and protocol.script-execution-stack.

sources.name: Optech Schnorr/Taproot Workshop
sources.scope: Focused advanced curriculum around Schnorr, Taproot, and related prerequisite chains.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the 12 module pages plus shared assets once per source.
sources.notes: Best current source for missing Taproot bridge concepts such as tagged hashes, taptweak, key-path spends, tapleaf inclusion proofs, Huffman-optimized TapTrees, and concrete policy case studies like degrading multisig. Implemented from this source in the current branch: protocol.tagged-hashes, protocol.taptweak, protocol.taproot-key-path-spends, protocol.taptree, protocol.tapleaf-inclusion-proofs, protocol.huffman-optimized-taptrees, and custody.degrading-multisig.

sources.name: Decoding by Bitcoin Devs
sources.scope: Structured educational material that may reveal curriculum-style prerequisite bundles.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the Introduction, Tools, Transactions, Scripts, and Taproot module groups before lesson-level expansion.
sources.notes: Best used for sequencing and beginner-friendly prerequisite ordering rather than broad topic discovery. Implemented from this source in the current branch: protocol.p2pk, security.nonce-reuse-attack, protocol.p2pkh, protocol.fee-calculation, protocol.op-checkmultisig, protocol.p2ms, protocol.bare-multisig-standardness, and protocol.musig.

sources.name: Programming Lightning
sources.scope: Interactive Lightning curriculum covering payment channels, channel state updates, HTLCs, onion routing, routing economics, and visual Lightning simulations.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the Lightning Tutorial, Onion Routing Tutorial, and Visual Lightning surfaces as separate course clusters before drilling into individual lessons.
sources.notes: Added from user direction. The public app bundle clearly exposes Lightning Tutorial, Onion Routing Tutorial, and Visual Lightning as the main extraction surfaces, which should produce bridge concepts in the payment-channel and routing branches without depending on transcript-style provenance. Implemented from this source in the current branch: lightning.per-commitment-secrets, lightning.obscured-commitment-number, lightning.commitment-output-scripts.

sources.name: Cypherpunk Library Bitcoin-Adjacent Canon
sources.scope: Bitcoin-adjacent cypherpunk readings focused on privacy, electronic cash, monetary theory, and the ideological roots most likely to sharpen foundational and history nodes.
sources.clusterStrategy: Start with four clusters: cypherpunk manifestos, privacy and surveillance essays, eCash and digital-cash texts, and monetary-theory readings. Defer clearly non-Bitcoin-adjacent political or general-hacker texts unless they surface a direct graph gap.
sources.notes: Added from user direction. High-signal starting texts from the collection include A Cypherpunk's Manifesto, The Crypto Anarchist Manifesto, Your Secret Right to Cash, Protecting Privacy with Electronic Cash, The Beauty of eCash, The Cyphernomicon, Ideal Money and Asymptotically Ideal Money, and 21 Lessons. Implemented from this source in the current branch: history.chaumian-ecash, privacy.secret-right-to-cash. Merged into existing coverage in the current branch: history.cypherpunks via A Cypherpunk's Manifesto; history.bitcoin-anarchism via The Crypto Anarchist Manifesto.

sources.name: Bitcoin Dev Project Explained Posts
sources.scope: Short-form educational posts from @Bitcoin_Devs that explicitly explain protocol, wallet, and Lightning mechanisms in learner-facing language.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the live X search as one source-family row first, then expand to one row per matching post once current results are enumerated. Group matching posts by concept family such as hashing, fee management, node architecture, channel mechanics, and upgrade proposals.
sources.notes: Added from user direction. The live X query was enumerated in the logged-in in-app browser on July 8, 2026 and surfaced 43 matching posts. Visible examples include Lamport Signature explained, Duplicate transaction bug explained, 64-byte transaction bug explained, SegWit Address explained, Off-by-one difficulty bug explained, Great Consensus Cleanup explained Part 1: Time Warp Attack, Transaction Pinning explained, Full Node Architecture Explained, MAST explained simply, BIP 158 explained, MuSig explained Simply, CPFP transaction explained, CISA explained, and BIP-68 Explained. Implemented from this source in the current branch: protocol.time-warp-attack, protocol.duplicate-transaction-bug, protocol.64-byte-transaction-bug. Merged into existing coverage in the current branch: protocol.time-warp-attack via Off-by-one difficulty bug explained.

sources.name: Bitcoin Core Academy Wallet Internals
sources.scope: Wallet internals cluster around ScriptPubKeyManagers, wallet keys, transaction identification, and balance calculation.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track one wallet-internals page cluster at a time so Core-specific abstractions stay atomic instead of collapsing into generic wallet concepts.
sources.notes: First additional-source pass beyond the mandatory seed list. The current additive winner is dev.scriptpubkeymanagers; wallet-encryption remains deferred because the cleanest surviving slice is still too coupled to missing Core wallet-internals parents.

sources.name: Bitcoin Core Academy Mempool Mechanics
sources.scope: Mempool and relay internals cluster around transaction relay, rebroadcast behavior, and local node transaction state.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the academy mempool pages as a separate cluster so implementation-specific relay mechanisms are not collapsed into generic mempool coverage.
sources.notes: Second post-seed-source slice. The current additive winners are protocol.mempool-unbroadcast-set, protocol.transaction-rebroadcast-privacy, and protocol.txid-vs-wtxid. The remaining transaction-format internals are now mostly implementation-detail material rather than clean learner-facing nodes.

sources.name: Bitcoin Design Payment Request Formats
sources.scope: User-facing payment request and address-sharing concepts across human-readable addresses, transaction display, and wallet privacy tradeoffs.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track design guide pages by UX concept cluster so user-facing payment abstractions stay separate from protocol internals and generic wallet hygiene.
sources.notes: First Bitcoin Design implementation slice. The current additive winner is custody.human-readable-addresses; encrypted cloud backups remains the main deferred neighbor from this design source family.

sources.name: Bitcoin Design Wallet Activity Context
sources.scope: User-facing wallet activity, contacts, and contextual payment-history concepts that help learners distinguish on-chain events from wallet-added meaning.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track activity and contacts pages together so contextual metadata stays separate from backup flows and from protocol transaction mechanics.
sources.notes: Second Bitcoin Design implementation slice. The current additive winner is custody.transaction-metadata; encrypted cloud backups remains the main deferred design-side candidate.

sources.name: Bitcoin Design Multiple Wallets
sources.scope: User-facing wallet architecture around separating funds by purpose, ownership, and security model inside one application.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the multiple-wallets page separately so purpose-based wallet separation stays distinct from account-path mechanics, backup flows, and generic wallet activity metadata.
sources.notes: Third Bitcoin Design implementation slice. The current additive winner is custody.multiple-wallets; encrypted cloud backups remains deferred because it overlaps too heavily with backups-recovery.

sources.name: Bitcoin Design Automatic Cloud Backup
sources.scope: User-facing backup scheme that stores encrypted wallet recovery data with an OS or cloud provider for low-friction restoration.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the conceptual cloud-backup page, the daily-spending reference flow, and the private-key-management overview together so the scheme can be deduped against existing backup and Lightning recovery coverage.
sources.notes: Reviewed and merged into existing coverage instead of minting a node. The enduring source value is backup-scheme tradeoffs that already fit custody.backups-recovery plus lightning.channel-backups.

sources.name: Bitcoin Design Upgradeable Wallet
sources.scope: User-facing wallet architecture for progressive security and staged upgrades between key-management schemes.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the upgradeable-wallet reference design separately so progressive security becomes its own concept instead of staying buried inside cloud-backup discussions or multiwallet archival flows.
sources.notes: Fourth Bitcoin Design implementation slice candidate. The current additive winner is custody.progressive-security, which survives dedupe more cleanly than automatic cloud backups or wallet-encryption.

sources.name: Bitcoin Design Shared Wallet
sources.scope: Reference design for co-managed wallets using multi-key schemes, provider-held recovery keys, and household approval policies.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the shared-wallet page together with the generic multi-key explanation so persona-specific multisig scenarios are deduped against existing custody mechanism nodes.
sources.notes: Reviewed and merged into existing coverage instead of minting a node. The enduring source value is shared-wallet examples for custody.multisig, custody.multiple-wallets, and custody.progressive-security.

concepts.title: Tagged Hashes
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 0.3 Tagged Hashes
concepts.atomicityRationale: A narrow cryptographic construction repeatedly used in Taproot and Schnorr material, distinct from generic hash functions.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the base hash-function mental model before namespace-separated tagged hashing makes sense.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as part of the first Taproot-internal bridge slice.

concepts.title: X-Only Public Keys
concepts.sourceContext: Topics index and Taproot-related topic pages
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is a specific key representation concept that bridges Schnorr math to Taproot output keys.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The concept depends on both general key knowledge and the Schnorr/Taproot context in which x-only keys matter.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as part of the first Taproot-internal bridge slice.

concepts.title: TapTweak
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 2.2 Taptweak
concepts.atomicityRationale: A concrete Taproot mechanism that is smaller than Taproot as a whole and useful for gap diagnosis.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The tweak mechanism is a Taproot-specific key derivation step that directly depends on Taproot context, tagged hashing, and x-only key encoding.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as part of the first Taproot-internal bridge slice.

concepts.title: Taproot Key-Path Spends
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop modules 2.0 Introduction to Taproot and 2.1 Segwit V1
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one specific Taproot spending mode: spending by signature against the tweaked output key without revealing a script path. It is narrower than Taproot overall, narrower than TapTweak as a key-derivation mechanism, and distinct from Tapscript's script-path execution rules.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The direct learner need is understanding that the spend authorizes against the tweaked Taproot output key. Schnorr signatures and the broader Taproot context are already inherited through TapTweak, while script-path concepts such as Tapscript and TapTree are sibling alternatives rather than prerequisites.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest remaining workshop-native Taproot spending-mode slice after ruling out broader wrappers and keeping script-path detail in the Tapscript and TapTree branches.

concepts.title: TapTree
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 2.4 Taptree
concepts.atomicityRationale: Specific Taproot script-tree structure, distinct from generic Merkle trees and from Taproot overall.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: TapTree is best taught immediately after TapTweak. Merkle-tree knowledge remains useful context but is already reached transitively through the Taproot path, so the direct edge would be redundant.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as part of the first Taproot-internal bridge slice.

concepts.title: TapLeaf Inclusion Proofs
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 2.4 Taptree
concepts.atomicityRationale: A concrete proof object within script-path spends, narrower than Taproot and narrower than TapTree.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The proof only makes sense after the learner understands the TapTree structure it proves against.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as part of the first Taproot-internal bridge slice.

concepts.title: Huffman-Optimized TapTrees
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 2.5 Huffman Construction
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is a narrow optimization concept that explains one specific design tradeoff inside Taproot trees.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The optimization is about arranging TapTree leaves, so the base tree concept is direct.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the last explicit pending workshop optimization concept.

concepts.title: Degrading Multisig
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 3.1 Degrading Multisig
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific timeout-based custody policy, narrower than generic multisig, narrower than threshold signatures, and narrower than broad inheritance or recovery planning.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the baseline multisig model and timelock semantics first. Taproot is a clean implementation path surfaced by the workshop, but the custody policy itself can be understood before committing to a specific script-path mechanism. Generic backup planning is a downstream use context rather than a prerequisite for understanding this script-policy construction.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the next workshop-derived custody case study, while leaving broader threshold-signature umbrella material for later and keeping Taproot as implementation context rather than a direct prerequisite.

concepts.title: Chain Split Risk
concepts.sourceContext: Upgrading chapter 5.3.1 Costs of a split
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific upgrade-risk concept about the costs of incompatible rule sets continuing in parallel, not a broad deployment-mechanisms umbrella.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the compatibility distinction between soft forks and hard forks before the consequences of a persistent split make sense.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as a narrow upgrade-risk bridge between protocol.softfork-hardfork and the existing activation-history case studies.

concepts.title: Tapscript
concepts.sourceContext: Topics alias normalization around Tapscript and Taproot script-path rules
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is the specific Taproot-era script ruleset, narrower than Taproot and cleaner than a broad script-path-spends explainer.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The direct learner need is the Taproot spend context. The base Script model is already inherited transitively through the SegWit and transaction-lifecycle path, so adding protocol.script directly would be redundant in the current graph.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the next additive Taproot bridge without rewiring older phase-locked descendants such as protocol.op-checksigadd.

concepts.title: Taproot PSBT Fields
concepts.sourceContext: PSBT topic page plus linked implementation notes around BIP371 support for Taproot inputs and outputs
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is the narrow PSBT extension surface for Taproot-specific fields, distinct from general PSBT workflows, descriptors, and MuSig-specific coordination formats.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the base PSBT exchange model and the Taproot spend model first. A direct dev.psbt-v2 edge would overfit the node because BIP371 extends both PSBTv0 and PSBTv2 rather than depending on v2 specifically.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as a narrow wallet-development bridge between generic PSBT workflows and Taproot-specific signing metadata.

concepts.title: Accidental Confiscation
concepts.sourceContext: Optech topic page on soft-fork designs that can accidentally make old funds unspendable
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific upgrade-risk failure mode about spendability loss under new soft-fork restrictions, not a broad activation or governance wrapper.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the soft-fork restriction model before this failure mode makes sense. The chain-split and standardness-policy branches add useful context but are not direct prerequisites for the core concept.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as a narrow upgrade-risk leaf after protocol.chain-split-risk, without forcing policy or activation-history rewires.

concepts.title: Coin Selection
concepts.sourceContext: Chapter 2 overview: making change and coin selection during wallet transaction construction
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific wallet decision layer about choosing which UTXOs fund a payment, narrower than wallet management and broader than any one privacy heuristic or RPC surface.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The direct learner need is fee pressure and transaction-size tradeoffs. The UTXO model is already inherited transitively through the fee-market branch, and manual coin control is a downstream application of coin selection rather than a prerequisite.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as a wallet-construction bridge that parents several existing privacy and funding leaves conceptually, without rewiring those older phase-locked nodes yet.

concepts.title: Change Outputs
concepts.sourceContext: Chapter 2 wallet transaction construction flow: making change before coin selection and later privacy heuristics
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific transaction-construction concept: returning excess input value to the spender as a new output. It is narrower than generic outputs and narrower than the later wallet-policy and privacy decisions built on top of it.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the basic output model first. Transaction-lifecycle is broader context already inherited in the output branch, and coin selection is a downstream wallet decision that depends on understanding change outputs rather than preceding it.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the missing bridge between addresses-outputs and the existing coin-selection and change-privacy leaves.

concepts.title: Taproot Descriptors
concepts.sourceContext: Output script descriptors topic page plus BIP386 tr() descriptor support for P2TR outputs
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates the tr() descriptor layer for Taproot outputs, narrower than generic descriptors and narrower than PSBT or MuSig workflow integrations.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the base descriptor grammar and the Taproot output model before Taproot-specific descriptor structure makes sense. Import workflows are downstream consumers rather than prerequisites.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as a clean descriptor-side Taproot leaf without reopening PSBT transport or MuSig workflow rewires.

concepts.title: Descriptor Wallet Policies
concepts.sourceContext: Output script descriptors topic page mention of BIP388 wallet policies for descriptor wallets, reinforced by the HWI topic's external-signer use of descriptors
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates the BIP388 wallet-policy abstraction, narrower than generic descriptor wallets and distinct from descriptor import or PSBT signing flows.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the descriptor grammar and HD key-derivation structure first. Import flows and PSBT transport are downstream consumers of the wallet-policy format rather than prerequisites for understanding the policy abstraction itself.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the clean BIP388 slice after concluding that the broader descriptor-wallets label would duplicate the existing descriptor-wallet-imports alias.

concepts.title: Descriptors in PSBTs
concepts.sourceContext: PSBT topic page plus output script descriptors topic updates about descriptor-aware signer and wallet coordination
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one interoperability concept: carrying descriptor information inside PSBT-based coordination flows, narrower than generic PSBT workflows and narrower than broad descriptor-wallet abstractions.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the descriptor model and the base PSBT coordination model first. PSBT v2 and wallet-policy formats are important adjacent evolutions, but they are downstream refinements rather than prerequisites for the general descriptor-in-PSBT idea.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the cleanest remaining Optech-side interoperability slice after ruling out MAST, threshold signatures, and scriptless multisignatures as broader or more duplicate-prone.

concepts.title: MuSig
concepts.sourceContext: Taproot module page MuSig, reinforced by the Optech MuSig topic page and related BTC Transcripts discussions
concepts.atomicityRationale: MuSig is one specific aggregated-signature protocol, narrower than generic threshold signatures and narrower than downstream MuSig2 descriptor workflows.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the Schnorr signature model first. Wallet-level multisig and Taproot are useful comparison or deployment contexts, but they are not required to make the core aggregation concept intelligible.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the next shared Decoding, Optech, and BTC Transcripts slice without broadening into threshold-signature umbrella material or MuSig2-specific wallet rewires.

concepts.title: Version 3 Transaction Relay
concepts.sourceContext: Topics alias normalization around TRUC and version 3 relay
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is a distinct relay-policy concept that should not be collapsed into the broader package relay bucket.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The core learner need is to understand the pinning problem that v3 relay is trying to constrain. In the current graph, the policy boundary is already inherited transitively through the transaction-pinning branch, so adding it directly would be redundant.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the next policy-leaf slice after adding protocol.consensus-vs-policy. Alias normalization remains mandatory here because Optech names and implementation jargon vary, and the direct policy-boundary edge was intentionally dropped after the redundancy check proved it was already inherited.

concepts.title: Validation Sovereignty
concepts.sourceContext: Trustlessness chapter and dont trust verify framing
concepts.atomicityRationale: A precise learner outcome about why running or relying on validation matters, not just a broad philosophy label.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: It is a narrower applied consequence of trustlessness and verification culture.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the philosophy-side bridge between trustlessness and practical self-validation.

concepts.title: Consensus vs Policy
concepts.sourceContext: Upgrade and split-risk discussion, reinforced by policy-heavy material in Mastering Bitcoin
concepts.atomicityRationale: A precise distinction that explains multiple later topics like relay, fee bumping, and upgrades.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the base idea of consensus rules before contrasting them with local relay policy.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch after the Taproot-internal slice. This is the cleanest conceptual parent for the existing policy and relay cluster.

concepts.title: Address Reuse Harms
concepts.sourceContext: Wallet, privacy, and address-format clusters; reinforced by philosophy privacy chapter
concepts.atomicityRationale: Narrow user-facing privacy consequence that is more actionable than a broad privacy umbrella.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The harm is best understood once the learner knows what addresses are and why pseudonymity is fragile.
concepts.notes: Do not add as a separate node. Fold any improved copy or resources into privacy.address-reuse.

concepts.title: Timelock Taxonomy
concepts.sourceContext: Authorization and transaction chapters covering CLTV, CSV, and sequence-based relative timelocks
concepts.atomicityRationale: A useful bridge concept that organizes multiple already-existing timelock mechanisms into one mental model.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The taxonomy should hang off the existing general timelock node rather than duplicate specific mechanism nodes.
concepts.notes: Do not add as a separate node. Enrich protocol.timelocks to make the taxonomy more explicit.

concepts.title: Light Client Trust Model
concepts.sourceContext: Network and blockchain chapters covering SPV, compact block filters, and lightweight-client tradeoffs
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is a specific tradeoff concept that bridges full-node verification to lightweight client behavior.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The trust model only makes sense once the learner can contrast full-node guarantees with filtered-client shortcuts.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as a thin conceptual bridge without rewiring older phase-locked neighbor nodes.

concepts.title: Full Node vs Lightweight Wallet
concepts.sourceContext: Introduction chapter discussion of full-node versus lightweight operating modes
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is a concrete learner decision point with one clear outcome: understand how wallet mode changes verification guarantees and trust assumptions.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need both the user-facing reason to self-verify and the mechanism-level trust tradeoffs before this comparison becomes meaningful.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the first downstream consumer for the light-client trust branch.

concepts.title: Nonce Reuse Attack
concepts.sourceContext: Taproot module page Nonce Reuse Attack
concepts.atomicityRationale: Specific failure mode inside Schnorr signing, small enough to diagnose as its own knowledge gap.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The learner needs the signature flow before the nonce leakage failure is meaningful.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the next Decoding-derived security slice.

concepts.title: Pay to Public Key (P2PK)
concepts.sourceContext: Scripts module page P2PK
concepts.atomicityRationale: Specific historical script pattern that should not be hidden inside broader script umbrella nodes.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: In the current graph, Bitcoin Script already brings the learner to the locking and unlocking model that makes a direct public-key output meaningful. Adding public-private-keys directly would duplicate that background transitively.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the cleanest Decoding-derived script-history slice.

concepts.title: Pay to Multisig (P2MS)
concepts.sourceContext: Scripts module page P2MS
concepts.atomicityRationale: Specific bare multisig pattern, narrower than generic multisig and distinct from P2SH-based multisig.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Bare multisig is the concrete script template built around OP_CHECKMULTISIG, so the opcode mechanism is the clean direct parent once it exists in the graph.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch after adding protocol.op-checkmultisig as its direct protocol-side parent.

concepts.title: OP_CHECKMULTISIG
concepts.sourceContext: Scripts module page P2MS
concepts.atomicityRationale: A specific opcode-level mechanism that is smaller than bare multisig as a whole and useful for diagnosing why legacy multisig scripts are structured the way they are.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The learner needs the base Script execution model before a multisignature verification opcode and its stack behavior make sense.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the mechanism bridge that parents protocol.p2ms.

concepts.title: Pay to Public Key Hash (P2PKH)
concepts.sourceContext: Scripts module page P2PKH
concepts.atomicityRationale: Specific legacy output template that is narrower than broad address or script overviews and distinct from both P2PK and P2SH.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The learner benefits from first seeing the simpler direct-public-key output and then adding the hash-based locking step that differentiates P2PKH.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the next Decoding-derived output-template slice.

concepts.title: Bare Multisig Standardness
concepts.sourceContext: Scripts module page P2MS, especially the standardness-versus-validity discussion
concepts.atomicityRationale: A narrow policy leaf about one specific script template's default relay treatment, distinct from both the bare multisig template itself and the broader concept of standardness policy.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the bare multisig template and the general idea of standardness before the special relay treatment of bare multisig becomes meaningful.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the first policy leaf under the new legacy multisig mini-subgraph.

concepts.title: Cluster Mempool
concepts.sourceContext: Topics index cluster mempool
concepts.atomicityRationale: The source confirms this is already a stable concept in the graph, so the work here is provenance and aliasing rather than a new node.
concepts.notes: Duplicate detected. Use the source as enrichment only.

concepts.title: Silent Payments
concepts.sourceContext: Topics index silent payments
concepts.atomicityRationale: Already present locally, so the source should improve aliases, provenance, or resources rather than create a new node.
concepts.notes: Duplicate detected.

concepts.title: Package Relay
concepts.sourceContext: Topics index package relay
concepts.atomicityRationale: Already in the graph. The new work is to map related sources and separate it cleanly from v3 relay and cluster mempool.
concepts.notes: Duplicate detected.

concepts.title: Miniscript
concepts.sourceContext: Topics index Miniscript
concepts.atomicityRationale: Already represented. The source is useful for enrichment and related-source mapping.
concepts.notes: Duplicate detected.

concepts.title: BOLT12 Offers
concepts.sourceContext: Topics index Offers
concepts.atomicityRationale: Existing node confirmed. The source is valuable for terminology normalization from Offers to BOLT12 Offers.
concepts.notes: Duplicate detected.

concepts.title: ScriptPubKey Managers
concepts.sourceContext: Wallet cluster: ScriptPubKeyManagers plus Keys in the Wallet
concepts.atomicityRationale: One specific Bitcoin Core wallet abstraction that owns scriptPubKeys, address derivation branches, and ownership tracking for a managed script family. This is narrower than generic wallet internals and narrower than descriptor syntax by itself.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The clean direct parent is the descriptor model that modern SPKMs manage. Address types still matter conceptually, but they are already inherited transitively through dev.descriptors, and watch-only wallets are a common downstream use case rather than a prerequisite for the abstraction itself.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the first additive concept from the post-seed source expansion pass.

concepts.title: Mempool Unbroadcast Set
concepts.sourceContext: Mempool cluster: Mempool unbroadcast set plus Transaction relay
concepts.atomicityRationale: One specific Bitcoin Core mempool mechanism for tracking locally submitted transactions that have not yet been confirmed as propagated. This is narrower than generic mempool policy and narrower than the full transaction relay pipeline.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the mempool as the local transaction pool and the inv/getdata relay flow that supplies or confirms network knowledge of transactions. dev.p2p-messages is already inherited transitively through protocol.transaction-relay-inv-getdata, so it would be redundant as a direct edge.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest next additive academy slice after scriptpubkey managers.

concepts.title: Human-Readable Bitcoin Addresses
concepts.sourceContext: Payment request formats cluster: Human readable addresses
concepts.atomicityRationale: One specific payment UX concept: readable handles that resolve to normal payment instructions through a lookup system. This is narrower than general address formats, narrower than LNURL, and narrower than reusable-address protocols like silent payments.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The clean direct parent is the privacy problem created by reusable payment identifiers. The underlying address model still matters, but it is already inherited transitively through privacy.address-reuse, while LNURL and silent payments remain neighboring implementations or downstream examples rather than prerequisites.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest first Bitcoin Design gap because it is highly user-relevant and bridges wallet UX with privacy/trust tradeoffs.

concepts.title: Transaction Metadata
concepts.sourceContext: Daily spending wallet cluster: Activity plus Contacts
concepts.atomicityRationale: One specific wallet-layer concept: contextual information such as names, notes, tags, and linked contacts attached to payments or activity rows. This is narrower than generic transaction history and narrower than UTXO labeling, because it focuses on user-facing meaning and portability rather than spend-control metadata alone.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the existing concept of wallet-applied labels plus the privacy idea that transaction history creates pseudonymous patterns. Human-readable addresses are a common input to richer metadata, but they are a neighboring UX mechanism rather than a prerequisite for understanding that wallet metadata exists and must be portable.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest next design-side slice because it adds a clear wallet-data concept without collapsing into generic backup flows.

concepts.title: Multiple Wallets
concepts.sourceContext: Bitcoin Design guide page: Multiple Wallets
concepts.atomicityRationale: One specific wallet-architecture concept: separating funds into distinct wallets for different purposes, owners, or security models inside one application. This is narrower than generic wallet UX, narrower than HD account-path mechanics, and narrower than backup strategy discussions.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners first need the operational idea that custody decisions shift responsibility and risk management onto the user. Multisig and watch-only wallets are important examples inside multi-wallet setups, and HD wallets can implement the structure, but those are neighboring wallet types or mechanisms rather than prerequisites for understanding purpose-based wallet separation itself.
concepts.notes: Chosen over encrypted cloud backups and wallet-encryption because the design page is really about custody architecture and purposeful wallet separation, not about backup transport or at-rest key protection.

concepts.title: Progressive Security
concepts.sourceContext: Bitcoin Design reference design: Upgradeable wallet
concepts.atomicityRationale: One specific custody-architecture concept: deliberately staging stronger key-management schemes as holdings, confidence, and risk increase. This is narrower than generic wallet security, narrower than any single scheme such as cloud backup or multisig, and narrower than multiple-wallet UI organization.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the concrete upgrade targets first: external signing devices and multi-key wallet schemes. Self-custody is already inherited through both, while generic backup hygiene is a neighboring concern that the progressive-security path evaluates rather than a prerequisite for understanding the staged-upgrade pattern itself.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleaner long-lived concept from the design material after recording automatic cloud backups as merge-only coverage and after keeping wallet-encryption deferred due overlap with existing passphrase and backup branches.

concepts.title: Transaction Rebroadcast Privacy
concepts.sourceContext: Bitcoin Core Academy mempool and relay cluster: rebroadcasting transactions
concepts.atomicityRationale: One specific relay-privacy concept: origin leakage created when nodes rebroadcast wallet transactions differently from ordinary peer transactions. This is narrower than general transaction relay, narrower than the unbroadcast set mechanism, and narrower than broad network privacy guidance.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the existing mechanism for tracking locally submitted transactions that may need later rebroadcast plus the privacy idea that transaction-origin metadata can link activity back to users. Generic relay mechanics and mempool behavior are already inherited transitively through the unbroadcast-set node.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleaner next Academy mempool slice after ruling out broader transaction-format and wallet-encryption candidates.

concepts.title: TXID and WTXID
concepts.sourceContext: Bitcoin Core Academy mempool cluster: transaction format in the mempool
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one specific identifier distinction: txid commits to the base transaction serialization while wtxid commits to the witness-inclusive serialization. That is narrower than SegWit as a whole, narrower than relay mode changes like BIP339, and narrower than a broad transaction-format wrapper.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the SegWit serialization split first because txid and wtxid only diverge once witness data is separated from the original transaction format. Malleability and relay behavior are important consequences and use cases, but they are not required to understand the identifier distinction itself.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest remaining Academy transaction-format bridge after checking that the distinction is not already owned by transaction malleability or WTXID relay.

concepts.title: Fee Calculation
concepts.sourceContext: Transactions module page Fee Calculation
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one learner-facing mechanism: how a transaction's fee is derived and interpreted. It is narrower than fee-market dynamics, narrower than transaction creation as a workflow, and distinct from RPC-specific estimation tooling.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Block weight and vbytes are the clean direct parent because practical fee calculation depends on relating an absolute fee to blockspace pricing units. The current graph already reaches UTXO accounting transitively through the block-weight branch, so adding protocol.utxo-model directly would be redundant. Change outputs are a frequent consequence of fee setting, but not a prerequisite for understanding the calculation itself.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest surviving Decoding transactions slice because broader pages such as Creating a Transaction and Signing Transactions remain too bundled, while fee-market and RPC nodes already cover downstream economics and tooling.

concepts.title: Bech32m Addresses
concepts.sourceContext: Keys and Addresses chapter, Bech32 Addresses section covering Bech32m
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one address-encoding concept: the checksum format used for native witness version 1 and higher addresses. It is narrower than broad address overviews, narrower than Taproot as a whole, and distinct from script-template nodes such as P2TR or SegWit output types.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the general idea that addresses encode spend templates and network context, and they need the Taproot witness-version upgrade that made Bech32m necessary in practice. SegWit remains useful background, but it is already inherited through Taproot and would be redundant as a direct edge.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest surviving book-side address-format gap because broader privacy wrappers remained too umbrella-like and this slice had a lower duplicate risk in the current graph.

concepts.title: Script Execution Stack
concepts.sourceContext: Authorization and Authentication chapter, Script Construction section on the script execution stack
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one specific mechanism: the stack-machine evaluation model behind Script. It is narrower than the umbrella Script node and broader than individual opcode leaves, which makes it a useful missing bridge concept.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners first need the general idea that Script defines locking and unlocking conditions. Once that umbrella is in place, the stack execution model is the direct mechanism layer needed before opcode-specific leaves such as OP_CHECKMULTISIG.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest next book-side bridge concept after the address-format slice because it strengthens the existing Script subtree without reopening a broad wrapper or duplicating an opcode leaf.

concepts.title: Scriptless Scripts
concepts.sourceContext: Adaptor signatures topic cluster and adjacent Optech note grouping threshold and scriptless constructions
concepts.atomicityRationale: The term names a family of signature-based contract constructions, but in the current graph it is still broader than one learner-facing mechanism. The atomic primitive already represented here is adaptor signatures.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: If a learner encounters the term, the cleanest direct concept boundary to ground it is adaptor signatures. The other neighboring nodes are downstream applications or supporting constructions rather than a cleaner parent concept.
concepts.notes: Do not add as a separate node. Preserve discoverability by folding the term into protocol.adaptor-signatures metadata and by keeping the downstream applications split into their own nodes.

concepts.title: Per-Commitment Secrets
concepts.sourceContext: Lightning Tutorial commitment-secrets lesson with exercises for building the commitment secret, deriving the per-commitment point, and producing commitment keys
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one specific Lightning channel mechanism: the per-state secret chain used to derive per-commitment points and commitment keys. It is narrower than commitment transactions as a whole and narrower than downstream revocation or force-close behavior.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the channel-state context from commitment transactions before the per-state secret chain is meaningful. The underlying hash and key-material concepts are already inherited transitively through the commitment branch, so they would be redundant as direct edges.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest first additive slice from Programming Lightning because it fills a precise mechanism gap between existing commitment-state and revocation nodes without reopening broader routing or HTLC wrappers.

concepts.title: Time Warp Attack
concepts.sourceContext: Great Consensus Cleanup explained, Part 1: Time Warp Attack
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one specific consensus exploit: manipulating timestamps across retarget periods to distort future difficulty. It is narrower than difficulty adjustment as a whole and narrower than general timestamp rules.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the retarget mechanism to understand the attack surface and the consensus timestamp rules to understand how miners can push time data far enough to exploit it. Neither parent makes the other redundant.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest first additive slice from the explained-post cluster because it adds a concrete consensus bug that is not already covered by the broader retargeting or timestamp overview nodes.

concepts.title: Chaumian eCash
concepts.sourceContext: Protecting Privacy with Electronic Cash paired with The Beauty of eCash
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one specific pre-Bitcoin digital-cash design family built around blind signatures and a trusted mint. It is narrower than the broad pre-Bitcoin-digital-cash survey and narrower than modern Bitcoin-backed ecash systems.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the cryptographic privacy mechanism to understand how the cash stayed anonymous and the double-spend problem to understand why a central mint was still required. The broader prehistory survey is helpful context but not a strict prerequisite for this specific system.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest first additive slice from the Cypherpunk Library cluster because current coverage only mentions DigiCash inside the broader pre-Bitcoin survey and covers modern Bitcoin-backed mints elsewhere.

concepts.title: A Cypherpunk's Manifesto
concepts.sourceContext: A Cypherpunk's Manifesto by Eric Hughes
concepts.atomicityRationale: The text is foundational and high-signal, but in the graph it still works best as a canonical primary source for the existing movement-level node rather than as a separate learner-facing concept boundary.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: If learners encounter the manifesto title, the cleanest direct grounding concept is the existing Cypherpunk Movement node. The cryptography background remains a prerequisite for the movement node itself and does not justify minting a new manifesto-level leaf.
concepts.notes: Merge into history.cypherpunks. Preserve discoverability through aliases and a primary-source resource instead of creating a separate manifesto node.

concepts.title: The Crypto Anarchist Manifesto
concepts.sourceContext: The Crypto Anarchist Manifesto by Timothy C. May
concepts.atomicityRationale: The text is foundational and high-signal, but in the graph it works best as a canonical primary source for the existing Bitcoin-and-anarchism history node rather than as a separate learner-facing concept boundary.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: If learners encounter the manifesto title, the cleanest direct grounding concept is the existing Bitcoin and Anarchism node. The manifesto sharpens that framing, but does not survive dedupe as a separate prerequisite leaf.
concepts.notes: Merge The Crypto Anarchist Manifesto into history.bitcoin-anarchism; preserve discoverability via alias and a source-page resource, with no standalone node.

concepts.title: Your Secret Right to Cash
concepts.sourceContext: Your Secret Right to Cash by Peter Van Valkenburgh
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one specific privacy and rights concept: cash as a historically private bearer instrument whose default secrecy and permissionlessness are being lost in digital, intermediated payment systems. It is narrower than general privacy tradeoffs and narrower than censorship resistance as a whole.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need historical context for how money shifted from bearer instruments to account-based systems, and they need the general concept of censorship resistance to understand why losing private cash changes power over payments. Neither parent makes the other redundant.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest next Cypherpunk Library additive slice because it captures the bearer-cash privacy and exclusion tradeoff directly without collapsing into the already crowded money-theory lane or duplicating generic KYC and pseudonymity nodes.

concepts.title: Obscured Commitment Number
concepts.sourceContext: Commitment-transactions module exercises ln-exercise-obscure-factor and ln-exercise-obscured-commitment
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one specific Lightning commitment-state mechanism: how the channel state number is hidden in transaction fields using an obscure factor. It is narrower than commitment transactions as a whole and narrower than broader revocation or HTLC workflows.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners first need the per-state key and secret machinery because the obscured commitment number is another state-indexing mechanism attached to the same commitment-state lineage. Commitment transactions remain useful background but are already inherited through lightning.per-commitment-secrets.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest next Programming Lightning slice because it captures a distinct BOLT 3 state-number encoding mechanism with lower dedupe risk than revocation, close, or HTLC wrapper lessons already covered.

concepts.title: Commitment Output Scripts
concepts.sourceContext: Commitment-scripts lesson exercises create_to_remote_script and create_to_local_script, plus static-remotekey and csv-purpose checkpoints
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one specific BOLT 3 mechanism layer: the to_local and to_remote output scripts used inside commitment transactions. It is narrower than commitment transactions as a whole and narrower than later close, revocation, or HTLC workflows.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the per-state key derivation machinery to understand where the revocation and delayed-payment keys come from, and they need CSV to understand the delayed to_local branch. Commitment-transactions remains useful context but is already inherited transitively through lightning.per-commitment-secrets.
concepts.notes: Add. Distinct BOLT 3 commitment-output script layer for to_local/to_remote and CSV delay; lower dedupe risk than revocation, close, or HTLC wrapper lessons already covered.

concepts.title: Duplicate Transaction Bug
concepts.sourceContext: Duplicate transaction bug explained: BIP 30 -> BIP 34 -> BIP 54
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one specific consensus bug: duplicated transactions with identical txids, especially duplicated coinbases, breaking the assumption that txids uniquely identify prior outputs. It is narrower than the broad double-spend problem and narrower than generic transaction malleability.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need coinbase-specific structure to understand why early blocks could replay special transactions and the UTXO reference model to understand why identical txids confused later spends. These parents stay direct without collapsing into broader history or transaction-lifecycle wrappers.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest next explained-post consensus-cleanup slice because it is distinct from existing double-spend and malleability nodes and has cleaner parents than DKG, Shamir, Lamport, or garbled-circuit candidates in the current graph.

concepts.title: 64-Byte Transaction Bug
concepts.sourceContext: Episode 5: The 64-byte transaction bug explained simply
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one specific Merkle-commitment failure mode: witness-stripped transactions whose 64-byte serialization can be mistaken for an internal Merkle-tree node preimage. It is narrower than generic Merkle-tree vulnerabilities and narrower than the full consensus-cleanup bundle.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the block header, transaction-merkle-root, and block-composition model before the internal-node ambiguity makes sense. That keeps the parent direct without expanding into a broader SPV, wallet, or hashing review chain.
concepts.notes: Add. Same explained-post consensus-cleanup lane; distinct Merkle-commitment failure with one clean direct parent and lower dedupe risk than DKG, Shamir, Lamport, or garbled-circuit candidates.

concepts.title: Off-by-One Difficulty Bug
concepts.sourceContext: Off-by-one difficulty bug explained
concepts.atomicityRationale: The phrase names the retargeting flaw that enables the time-warp exploit, but in the current graph it does not survive as a cleaner learner-facing boundary than the existing time-warp node.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: If learners encounter the off-by-one label, the cleanest direct grounding concept is the existing Time Warp Attack node, which already owns the exploit boundary and now absorbs the retargeting mechanism terminology.
concepts.notes: Merge into protocol.time-warp-attack. Preserve discoverability via aliases and a supporting Optech explainer instead of minting a duplicate consensus-bug node.
